title: Mobile operating system

infobox.value: is an [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") used for [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch"), [smartglasses](/wiki/Smartglasses "Smartglasses"), or other non-laptop [personal](/wiki/Personal_computing "Personal computing") [mobile computing devices](/wiki/Mobile_computing_device "Mobile computing device"). While computers such as [laptops](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") are "mobile", the operating systems used on them are usually not considered mobile, as they were originally designed for [desktop computers](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") that historically did not have or need specific *mobile* features. This "fine line" distinguishing mobile and other forms has become blurred in recent years, due to the fact that newer devices have become smaller and more mobile, unlike the [hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") of the past. Key factors blurring this line are the introduction of [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), light [laptops](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop"), and the hybridization of the [2-in-1 PCs](/wiki/2-in-1_PC "2-in-1 PC").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Operating system that operates a mobile device

For a list, see [Comparison of mobile operating systems](/wiki/Comparison_of_mobile_operating_systems "Comparison of mobile operating systems").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/1/14/Galaxy_S23_One_UI_8_Home_Screen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Galaxy_S23_One_UI_8_Home_Screen.jpg)

[One UI 8](/wiki/One_UI "One UI") on the [Samsung Galaxy](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy "Samsung Galaxy") S23 Ultra

A **mobile operating system** is an [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") used for [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch"), [smartglasses](/wiki/Smartglasses "Smartglasses"), or other non-laptop [personal](/wiki/Personal_computing "Personal computing") [mobile computing devices](/wiki/Mobile_computing_device "Mobile computing device"). While computers such as [laptops](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") are "mobile", the operating systems used on them are usually not considered mobile, as they were originally designed for [desktop computers](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") that historically did not have or need specific *mobile* features. This "fine line" distinguishing mobile and other forms has become blurred in recent years, due to the fact that newer devices have become smaller and more mobile, unlike the [hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") of the past. Key factors blurring this line are the introduction of [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), light [laptops](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop"), and the hybridization of the [2-in-1 PCs](/wiki/2-in-1_PC "2-in-1 PC").

Mobile operating systems combine features of a [desktop computer](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") operating system with other features useful for mobile or handheld use, and usually including a wireless built-in modem and [SIM](/wiki/SIM_card "SIM card") tray for telephone and data connection. In 2024, approximately **1.22 billion smartphones were sold globally**, marking a 7% increase over the previous year and a solid rebound after two consecutive years of declines.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Sales in 2012 were 1.56 billion; sales in 2023 were 1.43 billion[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) with 53.32% being [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)").[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Android alone has more sales than the popular desktop operating system [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), and smartphone use (even without tablets) outnumbers desktop use.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Mobile devices, with mobile communications abilities (for example, [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone")), contain two mobile operating systems. The main user-facing software platform is supplemented by a second low-level proprietary real-time operating system which operates the radio and other hardware. Research has shown that these low-level systems may contain a range of security vulnerabilities permitting malicious base stations to gain high levels of control over the mobile device.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Mobile operating systems have had the most use of any operating system since 2017 (measured by web use).[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

Mobile operating system milestones mirror the development of [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone"), [PDAs](/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant"), and smartphones:

### Pre-1990

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-1990")]

* 1990–2010 – [Mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") use [embedded systems](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system") to control operation.

### 1993–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1993–1999")]

* 1993
  + April – [PenPoint OS](/wiki/PenPoint_OS "PenPoint OS") by [GO Corp.](/wiki/GO_Corp. "GO Corp.") become available on the [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T_Corporation "AT&T Corporation") [EO Personal Communicator](/wiki/EO_Personal_Communicator "EO Personal Communicator").[[6]](#cite_note-PenPointOS-6)[[7]](#cite_note-PhabletHistory-7)
  + August – [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") launches [Newton OS](/wiki/Newton_OS "Newton OS") running on their [Newton](/wiki/Apple_Newton "Apple Newton") series of portable computers.
* 1994
  + March – [Magic Cap](/wiki/Magic_Cap "Magic Cap") OS by [General Magic](/wiki/General_Magic "General Magic") is first introduced on the [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") [Magic Link](/wiki/Magic_Link "Magic Link") [PDA](/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant").[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)
  + August – The first smartphone, the [IBM Simon](/wiki/IBM_Simon "IBM Simon"), has a touchscreen, [email](/wiki/Email "Email"), and PDA features.[[8]](#cite_note-SimonTurns20-8)
* 1996
  + March – The [Palm](/wiki/Palm,_Inc. "Palm, Inc.") [Pilot 1000](/wiki/Pilot_1000 "Pilot 1000") personal digital assistant is introduced with the [Palm OS](/wiki/Palm_OS "Palm OS") mobile operating system.
  + August – Nokia releases the [Nokia 9000 Communicator](/wiki/Nokia_9000_Communicator "Nokia 9000 Communicator") running an integrated system based on the [PEN/GEOS 3.0](/wiki/PEN/GEOS_3.0 "PEN/GEOS 3.0") OS from [Geoworks](/wiki/Geoworks "Geoworks").[[9]](#cite_note-NokiaCommunicatorPress-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Nokia9000Specs-10)
* 1997 – [EPOC32](/wiki/EPOC_(operating_system) "EPOC (operating system)") first appears on the [Psion Series 5](/wiki/Psion_Series_5 "Psion Series 5") PDA. Release 6 of EPOC32 will later be renamed to [Symbian OS](/wiki/Symbian_OS "Symbian OS").
* 1998 – [Symbian Ltd.](/wiki/Symbian_Ltd. "Symbian Ltd.") is formed as a joint venture by [Psion](/wiki/Psion_(computers) "Psion (computers)"), [Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson"), [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola"), and Nokia,[[11]](#cite_note-PsionHistory-11) Psion's [EPOC32](/wiki/EPOC_(operating_system) "EPOC (operating system)") OS becomes Symbian's EPOC operating system,[[12]](#cite_note-Nokia9210Press-12) and is later renamed to Symbian OS. Symbian's OS was used by those companies and several other major mobile phone brands, but especially Nokia.
* 1999
  + June – [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm")'s [pdQ](/w/index.php?title=Qualcomm_pdQ&action=edit&redlink=1 "Qualcomm pdQ (page does not exist)") becomes the first smartphone with [Palm OS](/wiki/Palm_OS "Palm OS").[[13]](#cite_note-QualcommPDQannounced-13)[[14]](#cite_note-QualcommPDQreleased-14)[[15]](#cite_note-pdQ800Specs-15)[[16]](#cite_note-pdQ1900Specs-16)
  + October – [Nokia S40](/wiki/Series_40 "Series 40") Platform is officially introduced along with the [Nokia 7110](/wiki/Nokia_7110 "Nokia 7110"), the first phone with [T9](/wiki/T9_(predictive_text) "T9 (predictive text)") [predictive text](/wiki/Predictive_text "Predictive text") input[[17]](#cite_note-17) and a [Wireless Application Protocol](/wiki/Wireless_Application_Protocol "Wireless Application Protocol") (WAP) browser for accessing specially formatted Internet data.[[18]](#cite_note-Nokia7110unveiled-18)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000s")]

* 2000 – The [Ericsson R380](/wiki/Ericsson_R380 "Ericsson R380") is released with EPOC32 Release 5, marking the first use on a phone of what's to become known as Symbian OS (as of Release 6).[[19]](#cite_note-EricssonR380PDAphone-19)[[20]](#cite_note-EricssonR380Specs-20)
* 2001
  + June – Nokia's Symbian [Series 80](/wiki/Series_80_(software_platform) "Series 80 (software platform)") platform is first released on the [Nokia 9210 Communicator](/wiki/Nokia_9210_Communicator "Nokia 9210 Communicator")[[21]](#cite_note-21) This is the first phone running an OS branded as Symbian, and the first phone using that OS that allows user installation of additional software.
  + September – [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm")'s [Binary Runtime Environment for Wireless](/wiki/Binary_Runtime_Environment_for_Wireless "Binary Runtime Environment for Wireless") (BREW) platform on their [REX](/wiki/REX_OS "REX OS") [real-time operating system](/wiki/Real-time_operating_system "Real-time operating system") (RTOS) is first released on the [Kyocera QCP-3035](/w/index.php?title=Kyocera_QCP-3035&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kyocera QCP-3035 (page does not exist)").[[22]](#cite_note-QualcommBREW-22)
* 2002
  + March
    - [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry_Limited "BlackBerry Limited") releases its first smartphone, running [Java 2 Micro Edition](/wiki/Java_Platform,_Micro_Edition "Java Platform, Micro Edition") (J2ME).[[23]](#cite_note-BlackBerryPhone-23)
    - [UIQ](/wiki/UIQ "UIQ") is first released, at v2.0,[[24]](#cite_note-UIQHistory-24) on [Symbian OS](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian"), and becomes available later in the year on the [Sony Ericsson P800](/wiki/Sony_Ericsson_P800 "Sony Ericsson P800"), the successor to the Ericsson R380.[[25]](#cite_note-P800HandsOn-25)
  + June
    - [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")'s first [Windows CE](/wiki/Windows_CE "Windows CE") (Pocket PC) smartphones are introduced.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)
    - [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia")'s Symbian [Series 60 (S60) platform](/wiki/S60_(software_platform) "S60 (software platform)") is released with the [Nokia 7650](/wiki/Nokia_7650 "Nokia 7650"), Nokia's first phone with a camera and [Multimedia Messaging Service](/wiki/Multimedia_Messaging_Service "Multimedia Messaging Service") (MMS).[[29]](#cite_note-29) S60 would form the basis of the OS on most of Nokia's smartphones until 2011, when they adopted Microsoft's [Windows Phone 7](/wiki/Windows_Phone_7 "Windows Phone 7"). S60 was also used on some phones from [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and others, and later by [Sony Ericsson](/wiki/Sony_Ericsson "Sony Ericsson") after the consolidation of some Symbian UI variants in 2008.
  + October – The [Danger Hiptop](/wiki/Danger_Hiptop "Danger Hiptop") (T-Mobile Sidekick in U.S.) is first released by [Danger, Inc.](/wiki/Danger_(company) "Danger (company)"), running [DangerOS](/wiki/Danger_Hiptop#Software "Danger Hiptop").
* 2003 – [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola") introduces first Linux-based cellphone [Motorola A760](/wiki/Motorola_A760 "Motorola A760") base on [Linux MontaVista](/wiki/MontaVista#Mobile_phones "MontaVista") distribution.
* 2005
  + May – Microsoft announces [Windows Mobile 5.0](/wiki/Windows_Mobile_5.0 "Windows Mobile 5.0").[[30]](#cite_note-30)
  + November – Nokia introduces [Maemo](/wiki/Maemo "Maemo") OS on the first, small Internet tablet, the [N770](/wiki/Nokia_770_Internet_Tablet "Nokia 770 Internet Tablet"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) with a 4.13" screen.
* 2007
  + January – Apple's [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") with iOS (named "iPhone OS" for its first three releases) is introduced as a "widescreen iPod", "mobile phone", and "Internet communicator".[[32]](#cite_note-Macworld2007Keynote-32)
  + February – Microsoft announces [Windows Mobile 6.0](/wiki/Windows_Mobile_6.0 "Windows Mobile 6.0").[[33]](#cite_note-33)
  + May – [Palm](/wiki/Palm,_Inc. "Palm, Inc.") announces the [Palm Foleo](/wiki/Palm_Foleo "Palm Foleo"), a "Mobile Companion" device similar to a [subnotebook](/wiki/Subnotebook "Subnotebook") computer, running a modified [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux") kernel and relying on a companion [Palm Treo](/wiki/Palm_Treo "Palm Treo") smartphone to send and retrieve mail, as well as provide data connectivity when away from WiFi.[[34]](#cite_note-PalmFoleoAnnounced-34) Palm canceled Foleo development on September 4, 2007, after facing public criticism.[[35]](#cite_note-PalmFoleoCanceled-35)
  + June – World's very first [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") is released in the United States.
  + November – [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance") (OHA) is established, led by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") with 34 members ([HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC"), [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"), [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel"), [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola_Mobility "Motorola Mobility"), [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics"), [LG](/wiki/LG_Electronics "LG Electronics"), etc.)[[36]](#cite_note-AndroidAnnouce-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* 2008
  + February – [LiMo Foundation](/wiki/LiMo_Foundation "LiMo Foundation") announces the first phones running the [LiMo](/wiki/LiMo_Platform "LiMo Platform") mobile Linux distribution, from [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola_Mobility "Motorola Mobility"), [NEC](/wiki/NEC_Corporation "NEC Corporation"), [Panasonic Mobile](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic"), and [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics"),[[38]](#cite_note-FirstLiMoPhones1-38)[[39]](#cite_note-FirstLiMoPhones2-39) released later in the year. The LiMo Foundation later became the [Tizen Association](/wiki/Tizen_Association "Tizen Association") and LiMo was subsumed by [Tizen](/wiki/Tizen "Tizen").
  + June – Nokia becomes the sole owner of [Symbian Ltd](/wiki/Symbian_Ltd "Symbian Ltd").[[40]](#cite_note-NokiaBuysOutSymbianLtd-40) The [Symbian Foundation](/wiki/Symbian_Foundation "Symbian Foundation") was then formed to co-ordinate the future development of the Symbian platform among the corporations using it, in a manner similar to the [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance") with Android.[[41]](#cite_note-SymbianFoundationFormed-41) Nokia remained the major contributor to Symbian's code.
  + July – Apple releases [iPhone OS 2](/wiki/IPhone_OS_2 "IPhone OS 2") with the [iPhone 3G](/wiki/IPhone_3G "IPhone 3G"), making available Apple's [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)
  + October – OHA releases [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") 1.0 (based on [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel")) with the [HTC Dream](/wiki/HTC_Dream "HTC Dream") (T-Mobile G1) as the first Android phone.[[44]](#cite_note-TalkAndroidG1Event-44)
  + November – Symbian^1, the [Symbian Foundation](/wiki/Symbian_Foundation "Symbian Foundation")'s touch-specific S60-based platform (equivalent to [S60 5th edition](/wiki/S60_5th_edition "S60 5th edition")) is first released on Nokia's first touchscreen Symbian phone, the [Nokia 5800 XpressMusic](/wiki/Nokia_5800_XpressMusic "Nokia 5800 XpressMusic"),[[45]](#cite_note-Nokia5800Press-45) with a resistive screen and a stylus.[[46]](#cite_note-Nokia5800Specs-46) Symbian^1 being derived from S60 meant that support for [UIQ](/wiki/UIQ "UIQ") disappeared and no further devices using UIQ were released.[[47]](#cite_note-UIQBankruptcy-47)
* 2009
  + January
    - [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") announces [Moblin](/wiki/Moblin "Moblin") 2, specifically created for [netbooks](/wiki/Netbook "Netbook") that run the company's [Atom](/wiki/Intel_Atom "Intel Atom") processor.[[48]](#cite_note-Moblin2-48) In April 2009 Intel turned Moblin over to the [Linux Foundation](/wiki/Linux_Foundation "Linux Foundation").
    - Palm introduces [webOS](/wiki/WebOS "WebOS") with the [Palm Pre](/wiki/Palm_Pre "Palm Pre")[[49]](#cite_note-PalmPreAnnounced-49) (released in June).[[50]](#cite_note-50) The new OS is not backward-compatible with their previous Palm OS.
  + February
    - [Palm](/wiki/Palm,_Inc. "Palm, Inc.") announces that no further devices with Palm OS are going to be released by the company.[[51]](#cite_note-NoMorePalmOSDevices-51) (The last was the [Palm Centro](/wiki/Palm_Centro "Palm Centro"), released October 14, 2007.[[52]](#cite_note-PalmCentroHandsOn-52))
    - [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") announces [Windows Mobile 6.5](/wiki/Windows_Mobile_6.5 "Windows Mobile 6.5"),[[53]](#cite_note-53) an "unwanted stopgap" update to Windows Mobile 6.1 intended to bridge the gap between version 6.1 and the then yet-to-be released Windows Mobile 7 (later canceled in favor of [Windows Phone 7](/wiki/Windows_Phone "Windows Phone")).[[54]](#cite_note-WM65stopgap-54)[[55]](#cite_note-BallmerWeScrewedUp-55) The first devices running it appeared in late October 2009.[[56]](#cite_note-ZDNetWM65devices-56)
  + May – [DangerOS](/wiki/Danger_Hiptop#Software "Danger Hiptop") 5.0 becomes available, based on [NetBSD](/wiki/NetBSD "NetBSD").[[57]](#cite_note-DangerNetBSD-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)
  + June – Apple releases [iPhone OS 3](/wiki/IPhone_OS_3 "IPhone OS 3") with the [iPhone 3GS](/wiki/IPhone_3GS "IPhone 3GS").
  + November – Nokia releases the [Nokia N900](/wiki/Nokia_N900 "Nokia N900"),[[59]](#cite_note-NokiaN900releasedUS-59) its first and only smartphone running the [Maemo](/wiki/Maemo "Maemo") OS intended for "handheld computers...with voice capability", while stating that they remain focused on [Symbian](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian") [S60](/wiki/S60_(software_platform) "S60 (software platform)") as their smartphone OS.[[60]](#cite_note-NokiaN900review-60) (Nokia had previously released three [Mobile Internet devices](/wiki/Mobile_Internet_device "Mobile Internet device") running Maemo, without cellular network connectivity.)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010s")]

#### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010")]

* February
  + [MeeGo](/wiki/MeeGo "MeeGo") is announced, a mobile Linux distribution merging Maemo from Nokia and [Moblin](/wiki/Moblin "Moblin") from Intel and Linux Foundation, to be hosted by Linux Foundation.[[61]](#cite_note-61) MeeGo is not backward-compatible with any previous operating system.
  + [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") introduces the [Bada](/wiki/Bada_(operating_system) "Bada (operating system)") OS and shows the first Bada smartphone, the [Samsung S8500](/wiki/Samsung_S8500 "Samsung S8500").[[62]](#cite_note-SamsungS8500Specs-62) It was later released in May 2010.[[63]](#cite_note-63)
* April
  + Apple releases the [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad") ([first generation](/wiki/IPad_(1st_generation) "IPad (1st generation)")) with [iPhone OS 3](/wiki/IPhone_OS_3 "IPhone OS 3").2.[[64]](#cite_note-iPadAvailable-64) This is the first version of the OS to support tablet computers. For its next major version (4.0) iPhone OS will be renamed iOS.
  + [HP](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard") acquires Palm in order to use [webOS](/wiki/WebOS "WebOS") in multiple new products, including smartphones, tablets, and [printers](/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)"),[[65]](#cite_note-HPacquiresPalm-65) later stating their intent to use it as the universal platform for all their devices.[[66]](#cite_note-HPwebOS-66)
* May – Microsoft [Kin](/wiki/Microsoft_Kin "Microsoft Kin") phone line with KIN OS (based on [Windows CE](/wiki/Windows_CE "Windows CE") and a "close cousin" to [Windows Phone](/wiki/Windows_Phone "Windows Phone")[[67]](#cite_note-KinOSWinPhone-67)) become available.
* June – Apple releases [iOS 4](/wiki/IOS_4 "IOS 4"), renamed from iPhone OS, with the [iPhone 4](/wiki/IPhone_4 "IPhone 4").
* July – Microsoft [Kin](/wiki/Microsoft_Kin "Microsoft Kin") phones and KIN OS are discontinued.[[68]](#cite_note-VerizonReturnsKins-68)[[69]](#cite_note-KinIsDead-69)
* September
  + Apple releases a variant of iOS powering the new [2nd generation Apple TV](/wiki/Apple_TV_(device)#Second_generation "Apple TV (device)").[[70]](#cite_note-iOSAppleTV-70)
  + [Symbian^3](/wiki/Symbian_OS "Symbian OS") is first released on the [Nokia N8](/wiki/Nokia_N8 "Nokia N8").[[71]](#cite_note-NokiaN8Shipping-71)[[72]](#cite_note-NokiaN8Specs-72) This would be Nokia's last flagship device running Symbian (though not their last Symbian phone), before switching to [Windows Phone 7](/wiki/Windows_Phone_7 "Windows Phone 7") for future flagship phones.
  + The [Danger Hiptop](/wiki/Danger_Hiptop "Danger Hiptop") line and [DangerOS](/wiki/Danger_Hiptop#Software "Danger Hiptop") are discontinued as a result of Microsoft's acquisition of [Danger, Inc.](/wiki/Danger_(company) "Danger (company)") in 2008.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* November
  + [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") assumes full control over Symbian as the Symbian Foundation disintegrates.[[74]](#cite_note-NokiaReabsorbsSymbian-74)
  + Windows Phone OS is released[[75]](#cite_note-75) on Windows Phone 7 [phones](/wiki/List_of_Windows_Phone_7_devices "List of Windows Phone 7 devices") by [HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC"), [LG](/wiki/LG "LG"), Samsung, and [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"). The new OS is not backward-compatible with the prior [Windows Mobile](/wiki/Windows_Mobile "Windows Mobile") OS.[[76]](#cite_note-Miniman-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

#### 2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2011")]

* February
  + [Android 3.0](/wiki/Android_version_history#v3.x_Honeycomb "Android version history") (Honeycomb), the first version to officially support tablet computers, is released on the Motorola [Xoom](/wiki/Motorola_Xoom "Motorola Xoom").[[78]](#cite_note-Android3Xoom-78)
  + [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") abandons the Symbian OS and announces that it would use Microsoft's Windows Phone 7 as its primary smartphone platform, while Symbian would be gradually wound down.[[79]](#cite_note-NokiaDropsSymbian-79)[[80]](#cite_note-RIPSymbian-80)
* April – [BlackBerry Tablet OS](/wiki/BlackBerry_Tablet_OS "BlackBerry Tablet OS"), based on [QNX Neutrino](/wiki/QNX_Neutrino "QNX Neutrino") is released on the [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry_Limited "BlackBerry Limited") [PlayBook](/wiki/BlackBerry_PlayBook "BlackBerry PlayBook").
* July
  + [Mozilla](/wiki/Mozilla "Mozilla") announces their *Boot to Gecko* project (later named [Firefox OS](/wiki/Firefox_OS "Firefox OS")) to develop an OS for handheld devices emphasizing standards-based Web technologies,[[81]](#cite_note-MozBoottoGecko-81) similar to webOS.
  + webOS 3.0, the first version to support tablet computers, is released on the HP [TouchPad](/wiki/HP_TouchPad "HP TouchPad").[[82]](#cite_note-webOS3-82)
* August – [HP](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard") announces that webOS device development and production lines would be halted.[[83]](#cite_note-HPkillswebOS-83) The last HP webOS version, 3.0.5, is released on January 12, 2012.[[84]](#cite_note-webOS305-84)
* September
  + [MeeGo](/wiki/MeeGo "MeeGo") is introduced with the limited-release [Nokia N9](/wiki/Nokia_N9 "Nokia N9"), Nokia's first and only consumer device to use the OS.[[85]](#cite_note-NokiaN9last-85) (A small number of the [Nokia N950](/wiki/Nokia_N950 "Nokia N950"), a MeeGo phone available only to developers, were released in mid-2011.[[86]](#cite_note-86))
  + After Nokia's abandonment of MeeGo, Intel and the Linux Foundation announce a partnership with [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") to launch [Tizen](/wiki/Tizen "Tizen"), shifting their focus from MeeGo (Intel and Linux Foundation) and [Bada](/wiki/Bada_(operating_system) "Bada (operating system)") (Samsung) during 2011 and 2012.[[87]](#cite_note-IntelSamsungLaunchTizen-87)
* October
  + Apple releases [iOS 5](/wiki/IOS_5 "IOS 5") with the [iPhone 4S](/wiki/IPhone_4S "IPhone 4S"), integrating the [Siri](/wiki/Siri "Siri") voice assistant.
  + The [Mer](/wiki/Mer_(software_distribution) "Mer (software distribution)") project is announced, based on an ultra-portable core for building products, composed of Linux, [HTML5](/wiki/HTML5 "HTML5"), [QML](/wiki/QML "QML"), and [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript"), which is derived from the MeeGo codebase.

#### 2012

## Current software platforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Current software platforms")]

See also: [Comparison of mobile operating systems](/wiki/Comparison_of_mobile_operating_systems "Comparison of mobile operating systems"), [List of iPhone models](/wiki/List_of_iPhone_models "List of iPhone models"), and [List of Windows phones](/wiki/List_of_Windows_phones "List of Windows phones")

These operating systems often run atop [baseband](/wiki/Baseband_processor "Baseband processor") or other [real-time operating systems](/wiki/Real-time_operating_system "Real-time operating system") that handle hardware aspects of the phone.

### Android

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Android")]

Main articles: [Android (operating system)](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), [Android version history](/wiki/Android_version_history "Android version history"), and [List of custom Android distributions](/wiki/List_of_custom_Android_distributions "List of custom Android distributions")

Android (based on the modified [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel")) is a mobile operating system developed by Open Handset Alliance.[[118]](#cite_note-OS_MS_3Q10-118) The base system is [open-source](/wiki/Free_and_open-source "Free and open-source") (and only the kernel [copyleft](/wiki/Copyleft "Copyleft")), but the apps and drivers which provide functionality are increasingly becoming [closed-source](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software").[[119]](#cite_note-119) Besides having the largest installed base worldwide on smartphones, it is also the most popular operating system for [general purpose computers](/wiki/Turing_machine "Turing machine")[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] (a category that includes desktop computers and mobile devices), even though Android is not a popular operating system for regular (desktop) [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") (PCs). Although the Android operating system is [free and open-source software](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_software "Free and open-source software"),[[120]](#cite_note-ICS_is_coming_to_AOSP-120) in devices sold, much of the software [bundled](/wiki/Product_bundling "Product bundling") with it (including Google apps and vendor-installed software) is [proprietary software](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") and closed-source.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

Android's releases before [2.0](/wiki/Android_Eclair "Android Eclair") (1.0, [1.5](/wiki/Android_Cupcake "Android Cupcake"), [1.6](/wiki/Android_Donut "Android Donut")) were used exclusively on mobile phones. Android 2.x releases were mostly used for mobile phones but also some tablets. [Android 3.0](/wiki/Android_Honeycomb "Android Honeycomb") was a tablet-oriented release and does not officially run on mobile phones. Both phone and tablet compatibility were merged with [Android 4.0](/wiki/Android_Ice_Cream_Sandwich "Android Ice Cream Sandwich").

Many OEMs ship their devices with [custom skins](/wiki/List_of_custom_Android_distributions "List of custom Android distributions") and numerous forks of Android are available to use as custom ROMs.

### Wear OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Wear OS")]

Main article: [Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS")

Wear OS (also known simply as Wear and formerly Android Wear) is a version of Google's Android operating system designed for smartwatches and other [wearables](/wiki/Wearable_computer "Wearable computer"). By pairing with mobile phones running Android version 6.0 or newer, or iOS version 10.0 or newer with limited support from Google's pairing application, Wear OS integrates [Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant "Google Assistant") technology and mobile notifications into a smartwatch form factor.

In May 2021 at [Google I/O](/wiki/Google_I/O "Google I/O"), Google announced a major update to the platform, internally known as Wear OS 3.0. It incorporates a new visual design inspired by Android 12, and Fitbit exercise tracking features. Google also announced a partnership with Samsung Electronics, who is collaborating with Google to unify its Tizen-based smartwatch platform with Wear OS, and has committed to using Wear OS on its future smartwatch products starting with [One UI](/wiki/One_UI "One UI") Watch 3.0. The underlying codebase was also upgraded to Android 11.

### ChromeOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: ChromeOS")]

Main article: [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS "ChromeOS")

ChromeOS is an operating system designed by Google that is based on the Linux kernel and uses the [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome") web browser as its principal user interface. As a result, ChromeOS primarily supports [web applications](/wiki/Web_application "Web application"). Google announced the project in July 2009, conceiving it as an operating system in which both applications and user data reside in the [cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing"): hence ChromeOS primarily runs [web applications](/wiki/Web_application "Web application").[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Due to increase of popularity with 2-in-1 PCs, most recent Chromebooks are introduced with touch screen capability, with Android applications starting to become available for the operating system in 2014. And in 2016, access to Android apps in the entire [Google Play Store](/wiki/Google_Play_Store "Google Play Store") was introduced on supported ChromeOS devices. With the support of Android applications, there are Chromebook devices that are positioned as tablet based instead of notebooks.

ChromeOS is only available pre-installed on hardware from Google manufacturing partners. An open source equivalent, [ChromiumOS](/wiki/ChromiumOS "ChromiumOS"), can be [compiled](/wiki/Compiled "Compiled") from downloaded [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code"). Early on, Google provided design goals for ChromeOS, but has not otherwise released a technical description.

### Sailfish OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sailfish OS")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article or section **may need to be [formatted](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Text_formatting "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Text formatting").** You can help Wikipedia by formatting it if you know how. Please also consider [changing this notice to be more specific](/wiki/Wikipedia:Template_messages/Cleanup#Structure,_formatting_and_sections "Wikipedia:Template messages/Cleanup"). *(March 2020)* |

Main article: [Sailfish OS](/wiki/Sailfish_OS "Sailfish OS")

Sailfish OS is from Jolla. It is open source with [GNU General Public License](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License") (GPL) for middleware stack core which comes from MER. Sailfish due to Jolla's business model and due to alliances with various partners and due to intentional design of OS internals, is capable to adopt in several layers third-party software including Jolla software e.g. Jolla's UI is proprietary software (closed source), so such components can be proprietary with many kinds of licences. However, user can replace them with open source components like e.g. NEMO UI instead Jolla's UI.

After Nokia abandoned in 2011 the MeeGo project, most of the MeeGo team left Nokia, and established Jolla as a company to use MeeGo and Mer business opportunities. The MER standard allows it to be launched on any hardware with kernel compatible with MER. In 2012, Linux Sailfish OS based on MeeGo and using middleware of MER core stack distribution was launched for public use. The first device, the [Jolla smartphone](/wiki/Jolla_(smartphone) "Jolla (smartphone)"), was unveiled on May 20, 2013. In 2015, Jolla Tablet was launched and the [BRICS](/wiki/BRICS "BRICS") countries declared it an officially supported OS there. Jolla started licensing Sailfish OS 2.0 for third parties. Some devices sold are updateable to Sailfish 2.0 with no limits.

[Nemo Mobile](/wiki/Nemo_Mobile "Nemo Mobile") is a community-driven OS, similar to Sailfish but attempting to replace its proprietary components, such as the user interface.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### SteamOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: SteamOS")]

Main article: [SteamOS](/wiki/SteamOS "SteamOS")

SteamOS is a [Linux distribution](/wiki/Linux_distribution "Linux distribution") developed by [Valve](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation"). It incorporates Valve's popular namesake [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") video game storefront and is the primary operating system for [Steam Machines](/wiki/Steam_Machine_(computer) "Steam Machine (computer)") and the [Steam Deck](/wiki/Steam_Deck "Steam Deck"). SteamOS is [open source](/wiki/Open_source "Open source") with some closed source components.

SteamOS was originally built to support streaming of video games from one [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") to the one running SteamOS within the same network, although the operating system can support standalone systems and was intended to be used as part of Valve's [Steam Machine](/wiki/Steam_Machine_(hardware_platform) "Steam Machine (hardware platform)") platform. SteamOS versions 1.0, released in December 2013, and 2.0 were based on the [Debian](/wiki/Debian "Debian") distribution of Linux with [GNOME](/wiki/GNOME "GNOME") desktop.[[126]](#cite_note-126) With SteamOS, Valve encouraged developers to incorporate Linux compatibility into their releases to better support Linux gaming options.

In February 2022, Valve released the [handheld](/wiki/Handheld_PC "Handheld PC") [gaming computer](/wiki/Gaming_computer "Gaming computer") [Steam Deck](/wiki/Steam_Deck "Steam Deck") running SteamOS 3.0. SteamOS 3 is based on the [Arch Linux](/wiki/Arch_Linux "Arch Linux") distribution with [KDE Plasma 5](/wiki/KDE_Plasma_5 "KDE Plasma 5").[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-:3-128)

### Tizen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tizen")]

Main article: [Tizen](/wiki/Tizen "Tizen")

Tizen (based on the Linux kernel) is a mobile operating system hosted by Linux Foundation, together with support from the Tizen Association, guided by a Technical Steering Group composed of Intel and Samsung.

Tizen is an operating system for devices including smartphones, tablets, [In-Vehicle Infotainment](/wiki/In-car_entertainment "In-car entertainment") (IVI) devices, however currently it mainly focuses on wearable and smart TVs. It is an open source system (however the SDK was closed-source and proprietary) that aims to offer a consistent user experience across devices. Tizen's main components are the Linux kernel and the [WebKit](/wiki/WebKit "WebKit") runtime. According to Intel, Tizen "combines the best of LiMo and MeeGo." [HTML5](/wiki/HTML5 "HTML5") apps are emphasized, with MeeGo encouraging its members to transition to Tizen, stating that the "future belongs to HTML5-based applications, outside of a relatively small percentage of apps, and we are firmly convinced that our investment needs to shift toward HTML5." Tizen will be targeted at a variety of platforms such as handsets, touch pc, smart TVs and in-vehicle entertainment.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) On May 17, 2013, Tizen released version 2.1, code-named Nectarine.[[131]](#cite_note-tizen2_1-131)

While Tizen itself was open source, most of the UX and UI layer that was developed by Samsung was mainly closed-source and proprietary, such as the TouchWiz UI on the Samsung Z's series smartphone and One UI for their Galaxy Watch wearable lines.

Note that some refrigerators use Tizen,[[132]](#cite_note-132) even though they are not considered mobile devices.

### KaiOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: KaiOS")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Please also consider [changing this notice to be more specific](/wiki/Wikipedia:Template_messages/Cleanup#Structure,_formatting_and_sections "Wikipedia:Template messages/Cleanup"). *(March 2025)* |

Main article: [KaiOS](/wiki/KaiOS "KaiOS")

KaiOS is from Kai. It is based on [Firefox OS/Boot to Gecko](/wiki/Firefox_OS "Firefox OS"). Unlike most mobile operating systems which focus on smartphones, KaiOS was developed mainly for feature phones, giving these access to more advanced technologies usually found on smartphones, such as app stores and Wi-Fi/4G capabilities.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

It is a mix of closed-source and open-source components.[[134]](#cite_note-support.kaiostech.com-134)[[135]](#cite_note-KaiOS/B2G_repository-135) FirefoxOS/B2G was released under the permissive [MPL 2.0](/wiki/Mozilla_Public_License "Mozilla Public License"). It does not redistribute itself under the same license, so KaiOS is now presumably proprietary (but still mostly [open-source](/wiki/Open-source "Open-source"), publishing its source code).[[134]](#cite_note-support.kaiostech.com-134)[[135]](#cite_note-KaiOS/B2G_repository-135) KaiOS is not entirely proprietary, as it uses the copyleft [GPL](/wiki/GPL "GPL") Linux kernel also used in Android.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

#### Smart Feature OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Smart Feature OS")]

### Fully open-source, entirely permissive licenses

#### Fuchsia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Fuchsia")]

Main article: [Fuchsia (operating system)](/wiki/Fuchsia_(operating_system) "Fuchsia (operating system)")

Fuchsia is a capability-based, real-time operating system (RTOS) currently being developed by Google. It was first discovered as a mysterious code post on GitHub in August 2016, without any official announcement. In contrast to prior Google-developed operating systems such as ChromeOS and Android, which are based on Linux kernels, Fuchsia is based on a new microkernel called "Zircon", derived from "Little Kernel", a small operating system intended for embedded systems. This allows it to remove Linux and the copyleft [GPL](/wiki/GPL "GPL") under which the Linux kernel is licensed; Fuchsia is licensed under the [permissive](/wiki/Permissive_licenses "Permissive licenses") [BSD 3-clause](/wiki/BSD_licenses#3-clause "BSD licenses"), [Apache 2.0](/wiki/Apache_2.0 "Apache 2.0"), and [MIT licenses](/wiki/MIT_license "MIT license"). Upon inspection, media outlets noted that the code post on GitHub suggested Fuchsia's capability to run on universal devices, from embedded systems to smartphones, tablets and personal computers. In May 2017, Fuchsia was updated with a user interface, along with a developer writing that the project was not a for experimental, prompting media speculation about Google's intentions with the operating system, including the possibility of it replacing Android.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

#### LiteOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: LiteOS")]

Main article: [LiteOS](/wiki/LiteOS "LiteOS")

LiteOS is a discontinued lightweight open source real-time operating system which is part of Huawei's "1+2+1" Internet of Things solution, which is similar to Google Android Things and Samsung Tizen. It was released under the [permissive](/wiki/Permissive_license "Permissive license") [BSD 3-clause license](/wiki/BSD_licenses#3-clause_license "BSD licenses"). LiteOS was used in the Huawei Watch GT series and their sub-brand Honor Magic Watch series.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### OpenHarmony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: OpenHarmony")]

Main article: [OpenHarmony](/wiki/OpenHarmony "OpenHarmony")

[OpenHarmony](/wiki/OpenHarmony "OpenHarmony") is an open-source version of HarmonyOS developed and donated by Huawei to the OpenAtom Foundation. It supports devices running a mini system with memory as small as 128 KB, or running a standard system with memory greater than 128 MB. The open source [HarmonyOS](/wiki/HarmonyOS "HarmonyOS") is based on the Huawei [LiteOS](/wiki/LiteOS "LiteOS") kernel and [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") for standard systems. OpenHarmony LiteOS Cortex-A brings small-sized, low-power, and high-performance experience and builds a unified and open ecosystem for developers. In addition, it provides rich kernel mechanisms, more comprehensive Portable Operating System Interface (POSIX), and a unified driver framework, Hardware Driver Foundation (HDF), which offers unified access for device developers and friendly development experience for application developers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Purism, in partnership with [GNOME](/wiki/GNOME "GNOME") and [KDE](/wiki/KDE "KDE"), aims to separate the [CPU](/wiki/Central_processor_unit "Central processor unit") from the [baseband processor](/wiki/Baseband_processor "Baseband processor") and include hardware [kill switches](/wiki/Kill_switch "Kill switch") for the phone's [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi"), [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), camera, microphone, and baseband processor, and provide both GNOME and [KDE Plasma Mobile](/wiki/KDE_Plasma_5#Plasma_Mobile "KDE Plasma 5") as options for the desktop environment.[[146]](#cite_note-LinuxMag_ChallengeFreePhone-146)[[147]](#cite_note-OSNews_security_privacy-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/UbuntuTouch-apps.png/250px-UbuntuTouch-apps.png)](/wiki/File:UbuntuTouch-apps.png)

Ubuntu Touch

Ubuntu Touch is an open-source ([GPL](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License")) mobile version of the [Ubuntu](/wiki/Ubuntu "Ubuntu") operating system[[112]](#cite_note-:0-112) originally developed in 2013 by [Canonical Ltd.](/wiki/Canonical_Ltd. "Canonical Ltd.") and continued by the non-profit UBports Foundation in 2017.[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) Ubuntu Touch can run on a pure GNU/Linux base on phones with the required drivers, such as the [Librem 5](/wiki/Librem_5 "Librem 5")[[140]](#cite_note-Fossbytes_UbuntuTouch-140) and the [PinePhone](/wiki/PinePhone "PinePhone").[[150]](#cite_note-150) To enable hardware that was originally shipped with Android, Ubuntu Touch makes use of the Android Linux kernel, using Android drivers and services via an [LXC](/wiki/LXC "LXC") container, but does not use any of the Java-like code of Android.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152) As of February 2022, Ubuntu Touch is available on 78 phones and tablets.[[112]](#cite_note-:0-112)[[153]](#cite_note-153) The UBports Installer serves as an easy-to-use tool to allow inexperienced users to install the operating system on third-party devices without damaging their hardware.[[112]](#cite_note-:0-112)[[154]](#cite_note-154)

### Closed source

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Closed source")]

#### iOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: iOS")]

Main articles: [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and [iOS version history](/wiki/IOS_version_history "IOS version history")

iOS (formerly named iPhone OS) was created by [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") It has the second largest installed base worldwide on smartphones, but the largest profits, due to aggressive price competition between Android-based manufacturers.[[155]](#cite_note-155) It is closed-source and proprietary, and is built on the open source [Darwin](/wiki/Darwin_(operating_system) "Darwin (operating system)") operating system. The iPhone, [iPod Touch](/wiki/IPod_Touch "IPod Touch"), [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"), and second and third-generation [Apple TV](/wiki/Apple_TV_(device) "Apple TV (device)") all use iOS, which is derived from [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS").

Native third-party applications were not officially supported until the release of iPhone OS 2.0 on July 11, 2008. Before this, "[jailbreaking](/wiki/IOS_jailbreaking "IOS jailbreaking")" allowed third-party applications to be installed. In recent years, the jailbreaking scene has changed drastically due to Apple's continued efforts to secure their operating system and prevent unauthorized modifications. Currently, jailbreaks of recent iterations of iOS are only semi-untethered, which requires a device to be re-jailbroken at every boot, and exploits for jailbreaks are becoming increasingly hard to find and use.

Currently all iOS devices are developed by Apple and manufactured by [Foxconn](/wiki/Foxconn "Foxconn") or another of Apple's partners.

It was announced at the company's 2019 Worldwide Developers Conference (WWDC), as a derivation from iOS but with a greater emphasis put on multitasking. It was released on September 24, 2019.

#### watchOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: watchOS")]

Main article: [watchOS](/wiki/WatchOS "WatchOS")

watchOS is the operating system of the Apple Watch, developed by Apple Inc. It is based on the iOS operating system and has many similar features. It was released on April 24, 2015, along with the Apple Watch, the only device that runs watchOS. It is currently the most widely used wearable operating system. It features focus on convenience, such as being able to place phone calls and send texts, and health, such as fitness and heart rate tracking.

On August 4, 2023, Huawei announces its full stack [HarmonyOS NEXT](/wiki/HarmonyOS_NEXT "HarmonyOS NEXT") for HarmonyOS that will replace the current multi-kernel stack that contains Linux kernel subsystem with APK apps, with only native HarmonyOS apps able to be used. On January 18, 2024, Galaxy Edition version was announced to be used for the next version of HarmonyOS.

#### Nintendo Switch system software

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Nintendo Switch system software")]

The [Nintendo Switch system software](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_system_software "Nintendo Switch system software") (also known by its codename Horizon) is an updatable firmware and operating system used by the [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") hybrid video game console/tablet and [Nintendo Switch Lite](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_Lite "Nintendo Switch Lite") handheld game console. It is based on a proprietary microkernel. The UI includes a HOME screen, consisting of the top bar, the screenshot viewer ("Album"), and shortcuts to the Nintendo eShop, News, and Settings.

The system itself is based on the [Nintendo 3DS system software](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS_system_software "Nintendo 3DS system software"), additionally the networking stack in the Switch OS is derived at least in part from [FreeBSD](/wiki/FreeBSD "FreeBSD") code while the Stagefright multimedia framework is derived from [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") code.

The [PlayStation Vita system software](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita_system_software "PlayStation Vita system software") is the official firmware and operating system for the [PlayStation Vita](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita "PlayStation Vita") and [PlayStation TV](/wiki/PlayStation_TV "PlayStation TV") video game consoles. It uses the LiveArea as its graphical shell. The PlayStation Vita system software has one optional add-on component, the PlayStation Mobile Runtime Package. The system is built on a Unix-base which is derived from FreeBSD and NetBSD.

#### Microsoft Windows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Microsoft Windows")]

Main articles: [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), [Windows 8](/wiki/Windows_8 "Windows 8"), [Windows 8.1](/wiki/Windows_8.1 "Windows 8.1"), [Windows 10](/wiki/Windows_10 "Windows 10"), and [Windows 11](/wiki/Windows_11 "Windows 11")

Windows is a personal computer operating system developed and released by Microsoft as part of the [Windows NT](/wiki/Windows_NT "Windows NT") [family](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows") of operating systems. It was released on November 20, 1985, but began focusing on tablet with the release of Windows 8 on October 12, 2012, after earlier attempts with editions based on Windows XP. It was designed to run across multiple Microsoft product such as PCs and tablets. The Windows user interface was revamped with Windows 8 to provide an improved tablet experience over the interface of Windows 7, including the introduction of modern Windows apps. Windows 10 would later combine the interface to provide a better experience for mouse-oriented devices and a touchscreen devices. Windows 10 also introduced the Universap Windows Platform, these apps can be designed to run across multiple Microsoft product families with nearly identical code‍ including PCs, tablets, smartphones, embedded systems, Xbox One, Surface Hub and Mixed Reality.

#### CyanogenMod

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: CyanogenMod")]

Main article: [CyanogenMod](/wiki/CyanogenMod "CyanogenMod")

CyanogenMod was a custom mobile operating system based on the Android Open Source Project (AOSP). It was a custom ROM that was co-developed by the CyanogenMod community. The OS did not include any proprietary apps unless the user installed them. Due to its open source nature, CyanogenMod allowed Android users who could no longer obtain update support from their manufacturer to continue updating their OS version to the latest one based on official releases from Google AOSP and heavy theme customization. The last version of the OS was CyanogenMod 13 which was based on Android Asus.

On December 24, 2016, CyanogenMod announced on their blog that they would no longer be releasing any CyanogenMod updates. All development moved to LineageOS.

##### Cyanogen OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Cyanogen OS")]

Cyanogen OS was based on [CyanogenMod](/wiki/CyanogenMod "CyanogenMod") and maintained by Cyanogen Inc; however, it included proprietary apps and it was only available for commercial uses.

#### Firefox OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Firefox OS")]

See also: [Comparison of Firefox OS devices](/wiki/Comparison_of_Firefox_OS_devices "Comparison of Firefox OS devices")

Firefox OS (formerly known as "Boot to Gecko" and shortly "B2G")[[156]](#cite_note-156) is from Mozilla. It was an open source mobile operating system released under the [Mozilla Public License](/wiki/Mozilla_Public_License "Mozilla Public License") built on the Android Linux kernel and used Android drivers, but did not use any Java-like code of Android.

According to [Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica"), "Mozilla says that B2G is motivated by a desire to demonstrate that the standards-based open Web has the potential to be a competitive alternative to the existing single-vendor application development stacks offered by the dominant mobile operating systems."[[157]](#cite_note-157) In September 2016, Mozilla announced that work on Firefox OS has ceased, and all B2G-related code would be removed from mozilla-central.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

#### MeeGo/Maemo/Moblin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: MeeGo/Maemo/Moblin")]

Main articles: [MeeGo](/wiki/MeeGo "MeeGo"), [Maemo](/wiki/Maemo "Maemo"), and [Moblin](/wiki/Moblin "Moblin")

[MeeGo](/wiki/MeeGo_(operating_system) "MeeGo (operating system)") was from non-profit organization [The Linux Foundation](/wiki/The_Linux_Foundation "The Linux Foundation"). It is open source and GPL. At the 2010 [Mobile World Congress](/wiki/Mobile_World_Congress "Mobile World Congress") in Barcelona, Nokia and Intel both unveiled *MeeGo*, a mobile operating system that combined Moblin and Maemo to create an open-sourced experience for users across all devices. In 2011 Nokia announced that it would no longer pursue MeeGo in favor of Windows Phone. Nokia announced the [Nokia N9](/wiki/Nokia_N9 "Nokia N9") on June 21, 2011, at the Nokia Connection event[[159]](#cite_note-159) in Singapore. LG announced its support for the platform.[[160]](#cite_note-160) Maemo was a platform developed by Nokia for smartphones and [Internet tablets](/wiki/Internet_tablet "Internet tablet"). It is open source and GPL, based on [Debian GNU/Linux](/wiki/Debian_GNU/Linux "Debian GNU/Linux") and draws much of its [graphical user interface](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") (GUI), [frameworks](/wiki/Software_framework "Software framework"), and [libraries](/wiki/Library_(computing) "Library (computing)") from the GNOME project. It uses the [Matchbox](/wiki/Matchbox_(window_manager) "Matchbox (window manager)") window manager and the [GTK](/wiki/GTK "GTK")-based [Hildon](/wiki/Hildon "Hildon") as its GUI and [application framework](/wiki/Application_framework "Application framework").

#### webOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: webOS")]

webOS was developed by Palm. webOS is an open source mobile operating system running on the Linux kernel, initially developed by Palm, which launched with the [Palm Pre](/wiki/Palm_Pre "Palm Pre"). After being acquired by HP, two phones (the [Veer](/wiki/HP_Veer "HP Veer") and the [Pre 3](/wiki/HP_Pre_3 "HP Pre 3")) and a tablet (the [TouchPad](/wiki/HP_TouchPad "HP TouchPad")) running webOS were introduced in 2011. On August 18, 2011, HP announced that webOS hardware would be discontinued,[[161]](#cite_note-161) but would continue to support and update webOS software and develop the webOS ecosystem.[[162]](#cite_note-162) HP released webOS as open source under the name Open webOS, and plans to update it with additional features.[[163]](#cite_note-163) On February 25, 2013, HP announced the sale of webOS to LG Electronics, who used the operating system for its "smart" or Internet-connected TVs. However, HP retained patents underlying WebOS and cloud-based services such as the App Catalog.

### Closed source

#### Bada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Bada")]

Main article: [Bada (operating system)](/wiki/Bada_(operating_system) "Bada (operating system)")

Bada platform (stylized as bada; Korean: 바다) was an operating system for mobile devices such as smartphones and tablet computers. It was developed by Samsung Electronics. Its name is derived from "바다 (bada)", meaning "ocean" or "sea" in Korean. It ranges from mid- to high-end smartphones. To foster adoption of Bada OS, since 2011 Samsung reportedly has considered releasing the source code under an open-source license, and expanding device support to include Smart TVs. Samsung announced in June 2012 intentions to merge Bada into the Tizen project, but would meanwhile use its own Bada operating system, in parallel with Google Android OS and Microsoft Windows Phone, for its smartphones. All Bada-powered devices are branded under the Wave name, but not all of Samsung's Android-powered devices are branded under the name Galaxy.
On February 25, 2013, Samsung announced that it will stop developing Bada, moving development to Tizen instead. Bug reporting was finally terminated in April 2014.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: BlackBerry OS")]

Services such as BlackBerry Messenger provide the integration of all communications into a single inbox. In September 2012, RIM announced that the 200 millionth BlackBerry smartphone was shipped. As of September 2014, there were around 46 million active BlackBerry service subscribers.[[165]](#cite_note-165) In the early 2010s, RIM underwent a platform transition, changing its company name to BlackBerry Limited and making new devices using a new operating system named "BlackBerry 10".[[166]](#cite_note-Kevin_McLaughlin-166)

In late 2016, BlackBerry announced that it will continue to support the OS, with a promise to release 10.3.3.[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-IDC.com-168) Therefore, BlackBerry 10 would not receive any major updates as BlackBerry and its partners would focus more on their Android base development.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Windows Mobile")]

Main article: [Windows Mobile](/wiki/Windows_Mobile "Windows Mobile")

Windows Mobile was a family of proprietary operating systems from Microsoft aimed at business and enterprise users, based on Windows CE and originally developed for [Pocket PC](/wiki/Pocket_PC "Pocket PC") (PDA) devices. In 2010 it was replaced with the consumer-focused Windows Phone.[[118]](#cite_note-OS_MS_3Q10-118)[[55]](#cite_note-BallmerWeScrewedUp-55)

Versions of Windows Mobile came in multiple editions, like "Pocket PC Premium", "Pocket PC Professional", "Pocket PC Phone", and "Smartphone" ([Windows Mobile 2003](/wiki/Windows_Mobile_2003 "Windows Mobile 2003")) or "Professional", "Standard", and "Classic" ([Windows Mobile 6.0](/wiki/Windows_Mobile_6.0 "Windows Mobile 6.0")). Some editions were touchscreen-only and some were keyboard-only, although there were cases where device vendors managed to graft support for one onto an edition targeted at the other. Cellular phone features were also only supported by some editions. Microsoft started work on a version of Windows Mobile that would combine all features together, but it was aborted, and instead they focused on developing the non-backward-compatible, touchscreen-only Windows Phone 7.[[76]](#cite_note-Miniman-76)

##### Windows Phone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Windows Phone")]

Main articles: [Windows Phone](/wiki/Windows_Phone "Windows Phone") and [Windows 10 Mobile](/wiki/Windows_10_Mobile "Windows 10 Mobile")

Windows Phone is a proprietary mobile operating system developed by Microsoft for smartphones as the replacement successor to Windows Mobile and [Zune](/wiki/Zune "Zune"). Windows Phone features a new touchscreen-oriented user interface derived from Metro design language.

Unveiled on February 15, 2010, Windows Phone included a user interface inspired by Microsoft's [Metro Design Language](/wiki/Metro_Design_Language "Metro Design Language"). It was integrated with Microsoft services such as [OneDrive](/wiki/OneDrive "OneDrive") and Office, [Xbox Music](/wiki/Xbox_Music "Xbox Music"), [Xbox Video](/wiki/Xbox_Video "Xbox Video"), [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live") games, and [Bing](/wiki/Microsoft_Bing "Microsoft Bing"), but also integrated with many other non-Microsoft services such as [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") and [Google accounts](/wiki/Google_Account "Google Account"). Windows Phone devices were made primarily by [Microsoft Mobile](/wiki/Microsoft_Mobile "Microsoft Mobile")/Nokia, and also by HTC and Samsung.

On January 21, 2015, Microsoft announced that the Windows Phone brand would be phased out and replaced with Windows 10 Mobile, bringing tighter integration and unification with its PC counterpart Windows 10, and providing a platform for smartphones and tablets with screen sizes under 8 inches.

On October 8, 2017, Microsoft officially announced that they would no longer push any major updates to Windows 10 Mobile. The operating system was put in maintenance mode, where Microsoft would push bug fixes and general improvements only. Windows 10 Mobile would not receive any new feature updates.[[113]](#cite_note-cnetW10MobileDead-113)[[114]](#cite_note-etW10MobileDead-114)

On January 18, 2019, Microsoft announced that support for Windows 10 Mobile would [end](/wiki/End-of-life_(product) "End-of-life (product)") on December 10, 2019, with no further security updates released after then, and that Windows 10 Mobile users should migrate to iOS or Android phones.[[116]](#cite_note-cnbcW10MobileEOL-116)[[117]](#cite_note-vergeW10MobileEOL-117)

### Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Usage")]

See also: [Usage share of operating systems § Crossover to smartphones having majority share](/wiki/Usage_share_of_operating_systems#Crossover_to_smartphones_having_majority_share "Usage share of operating systems"), and [Usage share of web browsers § Crossover to smartphones having majority share](/wiki/Usage_share_of_web_browsers#Crossover_to_smartphones_having_majority_share "Usage share of web browsers")

In 1992, the first smartphone prototype, the [IBM Simon](/wiki/IBM_Simon "IBM Simon"), was first showcased.[[171]](#cite_note-171)

The IBM Simon, was released commercially in 1994. It ran on the [Datalight ROM-DOS](/wiki/Datalight "Datalight") operating system.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

Between 1999 and 2002, Palm OS was the dominant player in the market, reaching 74% market share in 2000.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

Symbian overtook Palm OS as the leading player in 2003. It peaked at 73% market share in 2006. And starting losing shares thereafter but remained the largest player until 2012.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-:8-175)

In 2006, Android and iOS did not exist and only 64 million smartphones were sold.[[176]](#cite_note-Canalys_Q2-176) In 2018 Q1, 183.5 million smartphones were sold and global market share was 48.9% for Android and 19.1% for iOS. Only 131,000 smartphones running other operating systems were sold, constituting 0.03% of sales.[[177]](#cite_note-auto1-177)

According to [StatCounter](/wiki/StatCounter "StatCounter") web use statistics (a proxy for all use), smartphones (alone without tablets) have majority use globally, with desktop computers used much less (and Android, in particular, more popular than Windows).[[178]](#cite_note-178) Use varies however by continent with smartphones way more popular in the biggest continents, i.e. Asia, and the desktop still more popular in some, though not in North America.

The desktop is still popular in many countries (while overall down to 44.9% in the first quarter of 2017[[179]](#cite_note-Q1_2017-179)), smartphones are more popular even in many developed countries (or about to be in more). A few countries on any continent are desktop-minority; European countries (and some in South America, and a few, e.g. Haiti, in North America; and most in Asia and Africa) are smartphone-majority, Poland and Turkey highest with 57.68% and 62.33%, respectively. In Ireland, smartphone use at 45.55% outnumbers desktop use and mobile as a whole gains majority when including the tablet share at 9.12%.[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[179]](#cite_note-Q1_2017-179) Spain is also slightly desktop-minority.

The range of measured mobile web use varies a lot by country, and a StatCounter press release recognizes "India among world leaders in use of mobile to surf the internet"[[181]](#cite_note-181) (of the big countries) where the share is around (or over) 80%[[182]](#cite_note-182) and desktop is at 19.56%, with Russia trailing with 17.8% mobile use (and desktop the rest).

Smartphones (alone, without tablets), first gained majority in December 2016 (desktop-majority was lost the month before), and it was not a Christmas-time fluke, as while close to majority after smartphone majority happened again in March 2017.[[179]](#cite_note-Q1_2017-179)

In the week from November 7–13, 2016, smartphones alone (without tablets) overtook desktop, for the first time (for a short period; non-full-month).[[183]](#cite_note-183) Mobile-majority applies to countries such as Paraguay in South America, Poland in Europe and Turkey; and most of Asia and Africa. Some of the world is still desktop-majority, with e.g. in the United States at 54.89% (but no not on all days).[[184]](#cite_note-184) However, in some [territories of the United States](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States"), such as [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"),[[185]](#cite_note-185) desktop is way under majority, with Windows under 30% overtaken by Android.

On October 22, 2016 (and subsequent weekends), mobile showed majority.[[186]](#cite_note-186) Since October 27, the desktop has not shown majority, not even on weekdays. Smartphones alone have shown majority since December 23 to the end of the year, with the share topping at 58.22% on Christmas Day.[[187]](#cite_note-187) To the "mobile"-majority share then of smartphones, tablets could be added giving a 63.22% majority. While an unusually high top, a similarly high also happened on Monday April 17, 2017, with then only smartphones share slightly lower and tablet share slightly higher, with them combined at 62.88%.

According to a StatCounter November 1, 2016 press release[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mobile_operating_system&action=edit), the world has turned desktop-minority;[[188]](#cite_note-188) at about 49% desktop use for the previous month, but mobile was not ranked higher, tablet share had to be added to it to exceed desktop share. By now, mobile (smartphones) have full majority, outnumbering desktop/laptop computers by a safe margin (and no longer counting tablets with desktops makes them most popular).

Samsung released its last Tizen powered smartphone in 2017. Tizen peaked in that March at 0.25% market share.[[189]](#cite_note-189)

Firefox OS and Tizen both peaked in monthly market share in 2018 at 0.51% and 1.13% respectively.[[190]](#cite_note-:9-190)

Between 2018 and 2021, iOS and Android formed a mobile operating system duopoly, with no other OS reaching more than 1% market share for the full year.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

Microsoft ended support for its final mobile OS, Windows 10 Mobile, in January 2020.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

The COVID-19 pandemic led to a significant reduction in smartphone sales in 2020, with a 12.5% sales diminution year over year.[[193]](#cite_note-193)

2021 marked the apex of Android market share. Android reached 85% market share in quarter 3 2021, the highest ever recorded for a single mobile operating system[[194]](#cite_note-:5-194)

In February 2023, IIT Madras released BharOS, a GrapheneOS fork.[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196) Since release, BharOS has had a 0% market share.[[197]](#cite_note-:4-197)

Huawei announced [HarmonyOS Next](/wiki/HarmonyOS_5 "HarmonyOS 5") in August 2023. This was the first iteration of Harmony OS to not include the AOSP core. Harmony OS's market share prior to release was 2%.[[198]](#cite_note-198)

As of quarter 1 of 2025, Android was still by far the largest player with over three quarters, or 76%, of the market share. iOS remained solidly second with 19% of sales. Harmony OS reached 5% market share. This was the first mobile OS to reach the 5% milestone besides Android and iOS since Symbian 10 years prior.[[197]](#cite_note-:4-197)
